topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed on my screen, an unwelcome intrusion into my otherwise meticulously ordered afternoon. My thumb hovered over the 'details' button, a muscle twitching in my jaw. It was the address. My childhood home. Listed for sale. A wave of sourness washed over me, thick and unpleasant. I leaned back in my chair, the worn leather groaning in protest, and stared at the listing photos—the faded porch swing, the overgrown rose bushes—all rendered sterile and transactional by the real estate agent’s glossy filter. "Of course," I muttered, the sound dry in my throat. "The perfect place to liquidate memories." I scrolled through the price, a number that felt insulting, a final judgment on everything that had once been. I closed the browser tab with a decisive snap, the silence of my apartment suddenly deafening, punctuated only by the rapid drumming of my own pulse.

[story 2]

I picked up my phone, the screen illuminating my face with a cold, hard light. The listing was there, mocking me with its cheerful, professional layout. A slow, deliberate exhale escaped my lips, tasting like dust. I didn't need to read the description; I knew the architecture, the crooked window on the landing, the specific shade of peeling paint on the hallway wallpaper. It was all cataloged now, reduced to square footage and square dollars. I stood up, pacing the length of my living room, my footsteps sharp against the hardwood. My hands clenched into fists, the knuckles whitening. Why was this happening now? As if the mere existence of the property held some inherent shame. I walked to the window, looking out at the indifferent city skyline, feeling a profound detachment. This place, once the backdrop to my youth, was now just inventory.

[story 3]

The email landed in my inbox with a jarring chime. I opened it, and there it was: the address, the price, the professional photographs. A tight, unpleasant knot formed beneath my ribs. I didn't bother reading the finer print. My eyes skimmed the images, registering the familiar peeling paint and the slightly sagging roofline with a detached scrutiny. A low, almost inaudible scoff escaped my mouth. It was an ugly sound, rusty from disuse. I tossed the phone onto the sofa cushion, the impact barely registering. I stood by the door, arms crossed tightly over my chest, feeling a strange, brittle satisfaction settle over me. It was over, wasn't it? The sentimental residue had been officially priced and marked for disposal.
